metadata.split_role: Expanded gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, parasitic, and remaining oddball bolete records; second half of former mushrooms-boletes-other.json.
metadata.notes: Split from former mushrooms-boletes-other.json in v4.2.90 to reduce large-file handling/static. Record bodies and order were preserved; each canonical slug still appears in exactly one base mushroom split file. Physical data split only. Species record bodies were preserved; each canonical slug appears in exactly one base mushroom file. v4.2.81 added species-specific bolete notes/source links and simplified review reasons for selected records. v4.2.91: other-bolete review pass updated review flags using the 2-3 distinct-image rule; records with only one real image were kept or reopened for more distinct photo coverage, while records with 2+ distinct Commons/file images had stale image-only review flags cleared.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.distinct_image_rule: 2-3 image coverage means 2-3 genuinely different source images; thumbnails/detail/full variants or placeholders do not count.

records.culinary_uses: Not emphasized as a food mushroom; better kept as a confusing look-alike/comparison entry.

mushroom_profile.summary: Shaggy Strobilomyces species close to Old Man of the Woods; field ID is tricky and spores are the reliable separator.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; eastern North America.
research_notes: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black. Separating it from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods can require spore characters.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.
pore_color: Whitish pores bruising reddish then black

records.edibility_detail: Treat conservatively; ID can be difficult without microscopy.
records.mushroom_card_note: Shaggy Strobilomyces species close to Old Man of the Woods; field ID is tricky and spores are the reliable separator.
records.field_identification: Small erect blackish cap scales over pale gray ground, whitish pores that bruise reddish gray then black, and flesh that turns red then black. Separating it from S. floccopus / Old Man of the Woods can require spore characters.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Often considered edible, but this guide treats it as a careful-ID species rather than a beginner mushroom.

mushroom_profile.summary: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
research_notes: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Edible only with confident ID; gilled-bolete separation can be subtle.
records.mushroom_card_note: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
records.field_identification: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Often treated as edible, but keep the ID conservative because gilled bolete concepts are shifting.

mushroom_profile.summary: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
staining: Usually little to no bluing, occasional bluing reported
research_notes: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Edible with strong ID; not a beginner shortcut mushroom.
records.mushroom_card_note: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
records.field_identification: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.
records.photo_subject: Field-view fruiting body photos; duplicate thumbnail/detail/full variants do not count as extra coverage.
curation_notes: v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs are present; stale image-only review flags cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Not a high-value edible; better as an identification/comparison species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.
staining: Variable; generally not a strong blue-stainer
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Long slender stem, whitish pore surface becoming pinkish to pinkish-brown, and association with hemlock or other conifers. Older guides may call it Tylopilus gracilis.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
v4.2.91 image review: 3 distinct real image URLs present.

records.edibility_detail: Use cautiously; not treated as a prime table mushroom here.
records.mushroom_card_note: Slender hemlock-associated bolete with whitish pores that become pinkish, a long graceful stem, and a Tylopilus-like look.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target; useful as a Great Lakes specialty ID species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Great Lakes-region oak bolete with sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that brown strongly, and nearly round spores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, with Great Lakes records emphasized.
research_notes: Look for a sticky yellow-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface that bruises dark brown, a sheathing ring zone/veil remnant low on the stem, and oak association. Nearly round spores are a key confirmation feature.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
Needs real field photo / image coverage: no usable real image is currently loaded.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Parasitic Bolete. Added in the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass; fuller ID notes, sources, and images still need review.
staining: Little to no staining; may stain dirty brown slowly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, when earthballs are present.
research_notes: Species seed added from the Michigan / Upper Midwest bolete pass.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.

records.general_notes: Useful as a caution/curiosity species because the growth habit is distinctive: a bolete growing on another fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.field_identification: Small yellow-brown bolete that grows directly from common earthballs (Scleroderma), usually the most important ID feature. Pores are yellow to olive and the stem is small and often curved.
records.edibility_notes: The mushroom itself is sometimes described as edible, but this guide treats it as avoid/not recommended because of its poisonous earthball host.
curation_notes: Minimal species seed. Added during the bolete expansion pass for the clean app; details, seasonality, and images still need a later review.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-growing veined-pored Boletales oddball; keep it out of normal edible assumptions and use it as a form/comparison record.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where observed.
research_notes: Look for a wood-associated fruit body with a strongly veined to merulioid underside rather than normal round bolete pores. This is not a classic cap-and-stem bolete target.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
Needs more distinct photo coverage: only one real image is currently loaded.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bright yellow-veiled bolete when young, often with reddish/orange cap tones and blue-green bruising on pores.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; widespread but more southern.
research_notes: Young mushrooms have a powdery bright yellow veil covering the pore surface and stem; older specimens show yellow veil remnants, reddish to orange cap colors, yellowish pores, and greenish-blue bruising.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small sticky yellow-orange to reddish bolete with an unusually long, deeply reticulate stem; reported from Michigan but more southern/Appalachian in emphasis.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; recorded from Michigan to southeastern U.S.
research_notes: Check for sticky bright yellow/orange to reddish cap, yellow non-bruising pores, and a striking long stem that is deeply ridged or lacerate-reticulate. Oak or mixed oak-pine association.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
Needs real field photo / image coverage: no usable real image is currently loaded.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern oak-forest bolete with sticky pinkish-brown cap and bright yellow non-bruising pores; cap surface may taste sour.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America.
research_notes: Look for a sticky pinkish-brown to reddish-brown cap, bright yellow pore surface that does not bruise, white basal mycelium, and slender reddish/yellowish stem. Compare with B. innixus.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus tied to conifer wood and possibly Phaeolus schweinitzii; keep as an oddball ID record.
research_notes: Yellow-brown cap, yellow pore surface, wood association, and possible relationship with the dyer’s polypore Phaeolus schweinitzii are the useful clues. Compare with B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.
Source links and species-specific notes added in v4.2.81 mushroom batch.
